title: Understanding the Dynamics of Rotating Storms

lesson.learn: Tornadoes are complex and destructive weather phenomena characterized by intense, rotating columns of air. Forming during severe thunderstorms, they can cause catastrophic damage and loss of life. To better comprehend these events, it's essential to understand the physics involved, including wind shear, atmospheric instability, and the role of moisture. Recent research suggests that the combination of these factors can create an environment conducive to tornado formation.
lesson.simpler: Imagine two high-speed jets of air meeting in the sky, creating a rotating updraft. This phenomenon, known as a mesocyclone, can extend several miles into the atmosphere. As it touches the ground, it becomes a tornado, capable of causing significant damage and destruction.
lesson.keyTakeaway: One key factor in tornado formation is the presence of wind shear, which occurs when wind speed and direction change with height. This is often accompanied by instability in the atmosphere, leading to the development of updrafts and downdrafts. By understanding the interplay between these factors, researchers can better predict the likelihood of tornadoes.
lesson.deeperDive: According to studies published in the Journal of Applied Meteorology and Climatology, the Enhanced Fujita Scale (EF Scale) is currently the most widely used method for assessing tornado damage. The scale ranges from EF0 (light damage) to EF5 (incredible damage), with the most destructive tornadoes reaching wind speeds of up to 300 miles per hour. In the United States, Tornado Alley experiences an average of 53 tornadoes per year, with Oklahoma being one of the most affected states.
lesson.funFact: On March 18, 1925, a devastating tornado swept across parts of Missouri, Illinois, and Indiana, killing 695 people and injuring over 2,000. Known as the Tri-State Tornado, this event remains one of the deadliest in U.S. history, spanning 219 miles and lasting a staggering 3.5 hours.

people: - Dr. Tetsuya Fujita (Japanese meteorologist who developed the Enhanced Fujita Scale)
- Dr. Joanne Simpson (American meteorologist who contributed to understanding of tornado formation)
- Carl-Gustaf Rossby (Swedish-American meteorologist who pioneered research on atmospheric circulation)
places: - Joplin, Missouri, USA (site of devastating 2011 EF5 tornado)
- Norman, Oklahoma, USA (home to the National Weather Center and Storm Prediction Center)
years: - 1925: The year of the Tri-State Tornado
- 1998: The year the Enhanced Fujita Scale was introduced
- 2011: The year the devastating Joplin EF5 tornado struck
